title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: Credit Prices Continue to Weaken WoW; Type 1 Transfer Volumes Drop by Over 72%
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(15th â€“ 21st September 2025)
  • A total of 123,158 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 72.17% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 18.43% WoW as 618,992 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price stood at $53.65/MT for Type 1 transfers, recording a 3.37% fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers remained unchanged, priced at $55.98/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price declined by 5.09% WoW; on 23rd September, the spot price stood at $51.25/MT, and the benchmark price stood at $53.00/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices fell by 0.47% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices rose by 1.03% WoW, while D5 price rose by 1.04% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California Air Resources Board (CARB) has decided to conduct a public hearing to consider approving proposed amendments to the On-Road Heavy-Duty Engine and Vehicle Omnibus Regulation, the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS), and to propose permanently adopting the Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations that CARB previously noticed as emergency rulemaking. The CARB is proposing these Omnibus amendments, LCFS amendments, and permanent adoption of its Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations together as one rulemaking for administrative efficiency. The CARB staff has proposed to end the applicability of the transit agency diesel-fueled bus and engine exemption request process as well. It has planned to organize a virtual public workshop to discuss introducing gasoline containing up to 15 percent ethanol (E15) in California. It has intended to continue to accelerate zero-emission transportation with funding available through the Clean Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project (HVIP), the stateâ€™s primary incentive program for clean trucks and buses.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_604624" align="alignnone" width="760"] Table 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB.[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 3.37% WoW decline, falling from $55.52 to $53.65 this week, and all non-zero transfer prices remained unchanged WoW, priced at $55.98. Type 1 trades witnessed 123,158 credits exchanged, recording a 72.17% fall WoW. The total transaction volumes for all non-zero trades saw a decline of 18.4% WoW, reaching 618,992 credits. [caption id="attachment_604625" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption]

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_604626" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices fell by 5.09% WoW, reaching $51.85/MT. On 23rd September, the spot price stood at $51.25/MT. The benchmark price, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $53.00/MT as of 23rd September.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_604627" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 46 trades were reported in the market, with 3 high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $75.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 10,000 credits. The lowest transfer by price was $45.20 per credit for a Type 2 transaction reported for 1,166 credits. The largest volume of one transfer involved 200,000 credits for Type 2 transactions priced at $56.60. The smallest volume of transfer involved 1,158 credits for a Type 1 transaction priced at $47.50.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_604628" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average spot Type 1 price stood at $55.46 in September so far, falling from $56.47 in August. Trade volumes for the month of September stood at 2.02M as of 21st September, 2025.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_604629" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.Â Across 15th â€“ 21st September, total VPT remained unchanged WoW, with no change in all non-zero credit prices for the last week. The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN Weekly Price Trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices fell by 0.47% WoW. Both D4 and D6 prices rose by 1.03% WoW, while D5 price rose by 1.04% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming datesÂ 

Â Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this Week

  • Scoping Workshop on E15 Use in California by CARB: The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is organising a virtual public workshop to discuss introducing gasoline containing up to 15 percent ethanol (E15) in California. California gasoline is currently blended with up to 10 percent ethanol by volume (E10), the maximum ethanol content allowed by the California Reformulated Gasoline Regulations. At this workshop, CARB staff will provide an overview of the scientific evaluation of E15, current regulatory requirements, and concepts for potential regulatory updates as well as additional topics regarding the use of E15 in California.
  • California Wonâ€™t Replace Expiring $7,500 Federal EV Tax Credit: California will not backfill a $7,500 federal tax credit for buyers of electric vehicles that is set to expire this month, Governor Gavin Newsom said on Friday, reversing an earlier pledge to restart the stateâ€™s own EV subsidies. â€œWe canâ€™t make up for federal vandalism of those tax credits,â€ Newsom said during a press conference in San Francisco. The state will support expanding EV infrastructure, but â€œnot the direct subsidies that we cannot make up for,â€ the Democrat said. President Donald Trump campaigned on rolling back many of former President Joe Bidenâ€™s EV-friendly policies. Congress followed through in the major tax-and-spending bill, which placed a Sept. 30 expiration on the tax credit, ending years of federal support for EV buyers.
  • Proposed Amendments to the Heavy-Duty Engine, Low Carbon Fuel Standard Regulations and Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations: The California Air Resources Board (CARB or Board) will conduct a public hearing to consider approving proposed amendments to the On-Road Heavy-Duty Engine and Vehicle Omnibus Regulation, the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS), and to propose permanently adopting the Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations that CARB previously noticed as an emergency rulemaking on September 15, 2025.CARB is proposing these Omnibus amendments, LCFS amendments, and permanent adoption of its Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations together as one rulemaking for administrative efficiency, but the Omnibus and Associated Amendments, LCFS Amendments, and Emergency Vehicle Emissions Regulations are three separate regulatory items proposed for approval. CARB staff is also proposing to end the applicability of the transit agency diesel-fueled bus and engine exemption request process beginning with the 2027 MY.
  • Nearly 1 in 4 New Trucks, Buses and Vans in California Go Zero-Emission, 2 Years Ahead of Schedule: California is continuing to accelerate zero-emission transportation with funding available through the Clean Truck and Bus Voucher Incentive Project (HVIP), the stateâ€™s primary incentive program for clean trucks and buses â€” and demand is surging, with nearly $200 million requested on the first day applications opened. Funded mainly through Californiaâ€™s Cap-and-Invest Program, also known as Cap-and-Trade, the incentive program saw participation skyrocket 177% from 2023 to 2024, marking record demand for clean and zero-emission trucks and buses. In February of this year alone, more than 200 HVIP-funded vehicles hit the road with the help of $31 million in incentives â€” a pace that shows no signs of slowing in 2025.
